html:
This letter summarizes observations from the inspection of your sterile drug manufacturing facility completed on March 12, 2024.

Our inspection found repeated sterility assurance deficiencies related to aseptic processing, smoke studies, and environmental monitoring in Grade A and Grade B areas.

The media fill program did not adequately simulate worst-case operational conditions, and you failed to identify the root cause of positive units recovered during validation studies.

These practices cause your drug products to be adulterated within the meaning of section 501(a)(2)(B) of the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act.

We reviewed your quality system documentation for class II medical devices distributed under 21 CFR Part 820.

Design controls under 21 CFR 820.30 were incomplete because design inputs were not traced to verification and validation protocols for the infusion pump software.

Complaint records showed late trending of serious injuries and no evidence that corrective actions were validated before implementation.

Failure to establish and maintain adequate procedures causes devices to be adulterated under section 501(h) of the FD&C Act.

This warning letter addresses laboratory data integrity and good manufacturing practice observations at your finished drug product testing site.

Chromatography integration parameters were changed after injection without documented scientific justification, and original electronic raw data could not be retrieved for several stability study batches.

Shared administrator passwords for the LIMS allowed unidentified users to modify sample results, undermining ALCOA principles for laboratory records.

Until you correct these violations, FDA may withhold approval of new applications listing this facility.

FDA inspected your dietary supplement manufacturing establishment under 21 CFR Part 111.

Master manufacturing records did not list all components and did not specify in-process controls for each batch of capsules labeled as supporting immune health.

You did not establish specifications for identity tests on incoming botanical powders from overseas suppliers.

These deviations render your dietary supplements adulterated under section 402(a)(4) of the FD&C Act.

The inspection evaluated whether your cosmetic products are prepared, packed, or held under conditions that prevent adulteration as described in 21 CFR Part 700.

Water used in product formulation was not tested routinely for microbial contamination, and there was no written procedure describing sanitization of holding tanks.

Batch records for lot CG-88 omitted the identity and quantity of fragrance components blended into the finished product.

You must address these observations to ensure cosmetic safety and compliance.

This letter concerns Current Good Manufacturing Practice for Blood and Blood Components under 21 CFR Part 606.

Donor screening records showed missing vital signs for multiple collections, and deferral decisions were not consistently documented in the donor records.

Equipment used for plasma storage was not calibrated according to a written schedule, and temperature excursion logs were incomplete.

These failures may affect the safety and potency of blood products collected at your establishment.

FDA observed insanitary conditions during the inspection of your outsourcing facility registered under section 503B of the FD&C Act.

Environmental monitoring data for the ISO 5 hood showed recurrent mold recoveries without documented investigations or line clearance holds.

Stability studies supporting beyond-use dating for high-risk sterile preparations were not performed under the same container closure system used in dispensing.

Compounded drug products prepared under these conditions are adulterated within the meaning of section 501(a)(2)(A).

This correspondence addresses pharmacovigilance obligations for medicinal products for which you act as marketing authorization holder in partner territories.

Periodic safety update reports were submitted late for product X-100, and individual case safety reports for serious events lacked narrative summaries required by regional regulations.

Signal detection meeting minutes did not document medical review of disproportionality analyses for labeled adverse reactions.

FDA inspected your retail establishment for compliance with restrictions on flavored tobacco product sales under the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act.

Investigators observed covered tobacco products displayed within reach of customers without required age verification procedures at the point of sale.

Receipts and inventory records did not demonstrate that modified risk claims were removed from promotional materials as previously directed.

This letter concerns articles offered for import that appear to be misbranded and adulterated drugs under sections 502 and 501 of the FD&C Act.

Examination of shipment IMP-2024-09 found unapproved new drugs promoted for treating diabetes without adequate directions for use in English.

You did not register the foreign drug establishment or list the products with FDA as required under 21 CFR Part 207.

Future shipments may be subject to refusal of admission unless violations are adequately addressed.
